seoTitle: How to Set Correct Outlook SMTP Settings for Sending
metaDescription: Learn the correct Outlook SMTP settings for sending mail in 2026, including server hostnames, port 587, STARTTLS, and the shift to OAuth 2.0 auth.
intro: Outbound mail failures from third-party apps or website plugins usually come down to a mismatched SMTP configuration. This guide covers the exact server hostname, port, and encryption settings needed to send through Outlook, plus how Microsoft's 2026 move away from Basic Authentication affects your setup.

steps.heading: Identify your account type
steps.body: Free personal accounts use smtp-mail.outlook.com, while corporate profiles require smtp.office365.com.

steps.heading: Enter the server hostname
steps.body: Open your external email program's connection settings, navigate to the Outgoing Mail (SMTP) section, and paste in the correct server address.

steps.heading: Set the connection port
steps.body: Enter the port value 587 in the connection port field.

steps.heading: Enable encryption
steps.body: Select STARTTLS, or check the TLS/SSL upgrade box depending on your application's wording.

steps.heading: Turn on authentication
steps.body: Toggle the setting that requires authentication for the outgoing server to the enabled state.

steps.heading: Choose your credential method
steps.body: Select OAuth 2.0 as your validation method where supported; for legacy tools that can't process modern tokens, generate a dedicated App Password from Advanced Security instead, then send a test email to confirm delivery.

faq.question: Can I still use a regular password for SMTP?
faq.answer: Microsoft has retired legacy Basic Authentication for SMTP client submissions, so most setups now require OAuth 2.0 or a generated App Password.

faq.question: What port should I use for outgoing mail?
faq.answer: Port 587 with STARTTLS encryption is the standard configuration for Outlook's outgoing server.

faq.question: Why does my business account fail even with correct settings?
faq.answer: Your organization's administrator may need to enable SMTP AUTH client submissions at the tenant level for the connection to succeed.

faq.question: How do I know if my configuration actually works?
faq.answer: Send a test email from your third-party application and confirm it arrives without a server handshake error.
